title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
What is OBIS?


Ocean Biodiversity Information System

Global open-access data system for marine life

UNESCO Intergovernmental Oceanographic Commission

> 199 M occurrence records from >7,000 datasets

Data flows to GBIF — easy to publish once, reach both

Free to use. Free to contribute.


🌐 obis.org
📖 manual.obis.org


OBIS is endorsed by UNESCO’s IOC. Data published there can flow to GBIF as well, giving you double visibility with a single submission. Emphasize that OBIS is not just a repository but actively feeds into global biodiversity assessments, climate analyses, and conservation planning.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
A Mature Global Community







OBIS is the world’s largest open-access repositoryfor marine biodiversity data.
 

36 nodes  across 99 countries  representing 1,000+ institutions  and 6,000+ scientists.




OBIS sits within UNESCO’s IOC (IODE, est. 1961), working alongside ODIS for interoperability and OTGA for training. Strong partnerships include GBIF, TDWG, EMODnet, GOOS, and DataONE.
Current stats: 136M species observations, 12M records from ABNJ, 28M DNA sequences (Jan 2026).
This data supports multiple agreements: Paris Agreement, 30x30, GBF (Targets 3, 6, 8, 20, 21), SDG14, and now BBNJ.
Recognition: CBD COP16 recognized OBIS, GOOS, and ODIS as ocean components of the Global Biodiversity Observing System.
OBIS is listed in KMGBF Targets 20 and 21.
OBIS data supports: IPBES Global Assessment, UN World Ocean Assessments, UNESCO IOC Global HAB reports, State of the Ocean reports, TWAP Open Ocean component (UNEP), and CBD EBSA.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
Why publish eDNA data?



eDNA captures what other methods miss

Cryptic and rare marine taxa

Non-invasive, scalable sampling

Community-level biodiversity signals

Shared eDNA data is powerful data

Data contributes to global analyses
Data has long-term value
Your sequences become findable via OBIS sequence search tools





Benefit
Why it matters




Open DOI
Citable in publications


Long-term archive
FAIR data


Global reach
OBIS + GBIF


Sequence search
Reuse & reanalysis





Mention the OBIS sequence search prototype at sequence.obis.org — users can search for similar sequences across all OBIS datasets. This is only possible because sequences are published in standardized form.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
section: What is special about eDNA data?
text:
What is special about eDNA data?
Enormous potential…

Expands access to biodiversity data at scale
Captures environmental, specimen, and bulk-sample DNA
Raw sequences already widely shared in NCBI

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
section: What is special about eDNA data?
text:
What is special about eDNA data?

Special considerations
⚗️ Derived information — not a direct observation; extensive lab and bioinformatic processing
📚 Relies on reference databases — results depend on database completeness
❓ Large fraction of unknown sequences — no match in any database
📋 The DwC DNA Extension records how work was done, enabling future re-analysis


Raw sequences deposited in NCBI lack coordinates and dates — they cannot answer where or when a species was detected. OBIS, GBIF, and ALA now accept DNA-derived occurrences linked to space and time. DNA data requires special care: it is derived, depends on reference databases, and often produces many unknown sequences. The Darwin Core DNA extension preserves methodological context for future re-analysis.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
eDNA data standards

Two major eDNA data standards exist



MiXS  (Minimum Information about any (x) Sequence) — for raw sequences in NCBI



Darwin Core (DwC) Community vocabulary for biodiversity data — species, location, date, basis of record




OBIS + GBIF + ALA accept genetic data linked to spatial coordinates and time

 🔜Developing landscape

Rapidly changing field
For OBIS future directions include the DWC-DP and FAIReDNA terms


Raw sequences deposited in NCBI lack coordinates and dates — they cannot answer where or when a species was detected. OBIS, GBIF, and ALA now accept DNA-derived occurrences linked to space and time. DNA data requires special care: it is derived, depends on reference databases, and often produces many unknown sequences. The Darwin Core DNA extension preserves methodological context for future re-analysis.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
section: Darwin Core + DNA Derived Data Extension
text:
Darwin Core + DNA Derived Data Extension

Darwin Core: shared vocabulary for biodiversity data

DNA Extension: adds MIxS terms for sequences, primers, methods

Developed jointly by GBIF, OBIS, and the genomics community






meta.xml ← links all tables
eml.xml ← metadata (who, what, where, when, how)
occurrence.csv ← the occurrence records
dna_derived.csv ← the DNA extension



MIxS = Minimum Information about any (x) Sequence. The extension incorporates these community standards so the same fields used in INSDC/GenBank submissions can be reused here.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
The DNA publishing guide
Publishing DNA-derived data through biodiversity data platforms

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
Five DNA data categories
Which one fits your data?








#
Category
Example




1
DNA-derived occurrences
Metabarcoding (ASVs/OTUs assigned to taxa)


2
Enriched occurrences
Voucher specimen + barcoded


3
Targeted species detection
qPCR / ddPCR assays


4
Name references
Sequence in GenBank only


5
Metadata only
Dataset without sequences



🔎 Use the GBIF decision tree to confirm your category

We won’t go into details on this slide - just reiterating that Categories 1 and 3 are by far the most common for eDNA researchers. Category 1 covers metabarcoding; Category 3 covers targeted detection methods like qPCR/ddPCR where no sequences are produced. Be aware that there are other possible types but they are outside the scope of this workshop.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
From raw outputs → long format
DNA data is more complex than a lat/lon + species name
Your typical metabarcoding outputs:



File
Content




OTU/ASV table
Sequences × Samples (read counts)


Taxonomy table
Sequence [ID] → taxon assignment


Sample metadata
Location, date, method


FASTA file
Actual DNA sequences



The transformation:

One row = one unique sequence in one sample = one occurrence record


The most common stumbling block is this “wide to long” transformation. The OBIS manual has a clear diagram. The Metabarcoding Data Toolkit (MDT) can automate this for common pipeline outputs.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text: Example transformation

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
section: Key DwC fields to include: Occurrence
text:
Key DwC fields to include: Occurrence


Occurrence (core) table (mandatory fields)

occurrenceID — unique, stable ID
scientificName — WoRMS ID
basisOfRecord = “MaterialSample”

occurrenceStatus = “present”

If Occurrence core, also: eventDate, decimalLatitude, decimalLongitude

Occurrence table (strongly recommended fields)

scientificNameID (WoRMS AphiaID)

organismQuantity (read count of sequence)

associatedSequences (link to e.g. GenBank or ENA accession)
organismQuantityType = “DNA sequence reads”
sampleSizeValue (total read count in sample)

sampleSizeUnit = “DNA sequence reads”
samplingProtocol - (link to your SOP)

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
WoRMS Taxon Matching


Options for taxon matching:

WoRMS Taxon Match Tool
Programmatically:

obistools::match_taxa
worrms::wm_records_taxamatch
pyworms













No WoRMS match?


Use highest-rank taxon with a match (e.g. family or order). Keep the sequence — it can be re-identified later as reference databases improve.







Still no match?


Unknown sequences recorded as: scientificName = “Biota incertae sedis” scientificNameID = urn:lsid:marinespecies.org:taxname:12

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
section: Key fields to include: DNA Extension
text:
Key fields to include: DNA Extension


DNA Extension (example key fields)

DNA_sequence — ASV/OTU sequence
target_gene — e.g. “COI”, “18S rRNA”
pcr_primer_name_forward — and reverse
seq_meth — e.g. “Illumina MiSeq”
otu_class_appr — e.g. “DADA2 v1.18”
otu_db — reference database
sop — link to your protocol


 https://manual.obis.org/dna_data.html#quick-start-guide


WoRMS = World Register of Marine Species. Every marine occurrence should have a WoRMS AphiaID as the taxonID. For freshwater / terrestrial taxa use the appropriate backbone (CoL, etc.). Unidentified sequences should still be included — they can be assigned taxon later.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
Table structure options
Option A — Occurrence Core
occurrence.csv 
  └── dna_derived.csv  (linked via occurrenceID)
  └── emof.csv         (linked via occurrenceID)

Option B — Event Core (recommended for eDNA)
event.csv
  └── occurrence.csv        (linked via eventID)
       └── dna_derived.csv  (linked via occurrenceID)
  └── emof.csv              (linked via eventID or occurrenceID)
Why Event Core? Sample-level metadata (location, date, method) is recorded once per sampling event rather than repeated in every row.

For a survey with 500 samples × 2000 ASVs = 1M rows, repeating location and date for every row is wasteful and error-prone. The Event Core avoids this. A new GBIF/OBIS data model is under development that will be even more flexible but is not yet implemented (as of 2025).

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
Dataset Structure: Three Linked Tables



Event Core

eventID
eventDate
decimalLatitude
decimalLongitude
env_medium
samplingProtocol

→ the 📍where, 🕒when & 🧪how of collection



Occurrence Extension

occurrenceID
eventID
scientificName
taxonID (WoRMS)
basisOfRecord
occurrenceStatus

→ what organism was detected 🐠🪸🪼



DNA Derived Data Ext.

occurrenceID
DNA_sequence
target_gene
pcr_primer_forward
pcr_primer_reverse
seq_meth

→ 🧬the molecular context




Tables linked by eventID and occurrenceID


Optional 4th table: eMoF for measurements and facts


Your published dataset consists of up to three linked tables. The Event core captures sampling events — where, when, and how. The Occurrence extension records each taxon detection. The DNA Derived Data extension carries all molecular metadata. The tables are joined by eventID and occurrenceID. This avoids repeating sampling metadata for every sequence.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
section: Example usage of Event core structure
text:
Example usage of Event core structure



Why Event Core? Sample-level metadata (location, date, method) is recorded once per sampling event rather than repeated in every row.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
eMoF extension

Table to record all types of measurements (abiotic, biotic, environmental, sampling…)
Measurements recorded in long format

measurementType, measurementValue, measurementUnit

Linking sampling info to eventIDs decreases duplication
Use controlled vocabulary whenever possible!












eventID
occurrenceID
measurementType
measurementValue
measurementUnit





YEARsite1samp1

temperature
25
C












measurementTypeID
measurementUnitID





http://vocab.nerc.ac.uk/collection/P01/current/TEMPPR01/
http://vocab.nerc.ac.uk/collection/P06/current/UPAA/





As some of us may already be familiar, eMoF is the extension table that holds ALL kinds of measurement or fact data including measurements on organisms, information about the sampling gear used, or environmental variables measured. A reminder that this data is organized in LONG format. All the measurements are organized in rows within the columns measurementType, measurementValue, and measurementUnit

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
Controlled Vocabulary


Identifiers for each eMoF column

measurementUnitID
measurementValueID

non-numeric values

measurementTypeID

→ Facilitates data understanding
→ Enables data aggregation
→ Decreases misuse potential

What should vocabulary terms capture? 

OBIS recommends using NERC vocabulary terms



Emof lets you attach vocabulary identifiers to each of the emof columns. This makes our measurement data are more easily comparable and interoperable, more easily aggregated for analysis, and can decrease misuse potential!
When selecting a vocab it’s important to think about what information needs to be included in the definition for the users to understand your measurement so you should consider the property/attribute (e.g. length, concentration), what’s the object/entity of interest (a organism, a chemical), whether enviro context is important, and whether a user needs to know the methods used to properly understand the measurement OBIS recommends the NERC vocabular collection and the OBIS manual has much more detail on controlled vocabulary

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
Publishing workflow
Integrated Publishing Toolkits (IPT) are hosted by OBIS nodes — you do not need to run your own server. OBIS has nodes covering most world regions. Find yours at obis.org/nodes.




OBIS has nodes covering most world regions. Find yours at obis.org/nodes. The IPT has a built-in metadata editor for EML so you do not need to write XML by hand. Emphasize that once published, the dataset gets a DOI and can be cited in papers.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
Tools to Help You



GBIF Metabarcoding Data Toolkit
Automates DwC mapping from bioinformatic outputs
gbif.org/tools/mdt


robis (R package)
Query OBIS; retrieve DNA records with unnest_extension()
github.com/iobis/robis



obistools (R package)
Validation, QC, WoRMS matching, archive structure checks
github.com/iobis/obistools


OBIS Sequence Search
BLAST-search sequences already in OBIS (prototype)
sequence.obis.org



The GBIF Metabarcoding Data Toolkit takes your bioinformatic outputs and handles much of the Darwin Core mapping. The obistools R package checks coordinates, matches taxa to WoRMS, and verifies archive structure. The robis package lets you query OBIS and access DNA data with unnest_extension. The OBIS Sequence Search prototype lets you BLAST-search sequences already in OBIS.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
The Metabarcoding Data Toolkit (MDT)
A newer tool to simplify the workflow

Developed by GBIF specifically for metabarcoding data

Accepts common pipeline outputs (QIIME2, DADA2, etc.)

Handles the wide-to-long transformation

Generates Darwin Core Archive + DNA Extension automatically

Integrates with IPT publishing

🔗 Available at: mdt.gbif.org






Note


Still under active development — check the GBIF documentation for the latest capabilities. Currently not possible to use the event core structure




The MDT is a significant time-saver for complex datasets. It can be used alongside the IPT rather than replacing it — it handles the data formatting step and then hands off to the IPT for publishing.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
Quality control
Before publishing — check for:

✅ Taxonomy matched to WoRMS (AphiaID required for marine taxa)

✅ Coordinates in decimal degrees, in the ocean

✅ Dates in ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DD)

✅ Unique, stable occurrenceID and eventID

✅ Table linkages consistent (eventID, occurrenceID)

Tools:



Tool
What it checks




obistools (R package)
Taxonomy, geography, required fields


GBIF data validator
Darwin Core Archive structure


WoRMS taxon match
Scientific names → AphiaIDs




obistools is an R package from OBIS. The GBIF validator is a web-based tool. WoRMS has a batch taxon match tool at marinespecies.org/aphia.php. For unidentified sequences, assign a high-level taxon if possible (e.g., kingdom Animalia) rather than leaving the field empty.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
Resources and community



Resource
Link




OBIS Manual (DNA chapter)
manual.obis.org/dna_data.html


OBIS DNA training slides (this presentation!)
github.com/iobis/obis_edna_slides


GBIF DNA publishing guide
doi.org/10.35035/DOC-VF1A-NR22


OBON 2024 DNA training
github.com/iobis/obon-2024-dna-training


IOOS Bio Mobilization Workshop
ioos.github.io/bio_mobilization_workshop


GBIF-NA DNA Publishing Workshop
sunray1.github.io/2025-05-09-GBIF-NA-DNAPublishing


OBIS helpdesk
helpdesk@obis.org




The community is genuinely welcoming to beginners. The OBIS helpdesk responds quickly. The workshops listed here offer hands-on help with your own data — participants bring actual datasets and work through formatting with instructors.

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
OBIS DNA Data Management Platform
🔧 OBIS is developing a data management platform for DNA data including:



Bioinformatics
QC
Validation
Publication to OBIS pipeline

To be offered to OBIS nodes to support local eDNA projects!

🚧

title: Publishing eDNA Data in OBIS
text:
Summary


Three steps to publish your eDNA data:

Format — Darwin Core + DNA Derived Data Extension
Long format: one row = one sequence per sample
Document — Complete EML metadata
Primers, methods, bioinformatics pipeline, license
Publish — Through your OBIS node IPT
Get a DOI. Be cited. Join the global record.


Start here → manual.obis.org/dna_data.html

Why it matters

eDNA captures biodiversity no other method can.
Publishing it in OBIS makes it
reusable, citable, and permanent.



Close with energy. Remind the audience that the gap between eDNA data currently in OBIS and what could be there is enormous — their data can meaningfully fill it. If time allows, invite them to share what kind of eDNA data they have and talk about the right category/structure for their case.

title: Types of DNA-derived data
text:
Five DNA data categories
Which one fits your data?








#
Category
Example




1
DNA-derived occurrences
Metabarcoding (ASVs/OTUs assigned to taxa)


2
Enriched occurrences
Voucher specimen + barcoded


3
Targeted species detection
qPCR / ddPCR assays


4
Name references
Sequence in GenBank only


5
Metadata only
Dataset without sequences



🔎 Use the GBIF decision tree to confirm your category

We won’t go into details on this slide - just reiterating that Categories 1 and 3 are by far the most common for eDNA researchers. Category 1 covers metabarcoding; Category 3 covers targeted detection methods like qPCR/ddPCR where no sequences are produced. Be aware that there are other possible types but they are outside the scope of this workshop.

title: Types of DNA-derived data
text:
eDNA data standards

Two major eDNA data standards exist



MiXS  (Minimum Information about any (x) Sequence) — for raw sequences in NCBI



Darwin Core (DwC) Community vocabulary for biodiversity data — species, location, date, basis of record




OBIS + GBIF + ALA accept genetic data linked to spatial coordinates and time

 🔜Developing landscape

Rapidly changing field
For OBIS future directions include the DWC-DP and FAIReDNA terms


Raw sequences deposited in NCBI lack coordinates and dates — they cannot answer where or when a species was detected. OBIS, GBIF, and ALA now accept DNA-derived occurrences linked to space and time. DNA data requires special care: it is derived, depends on reference databases, and often produces many unknown sequences. The Darwin Core DNA extension preserves methodological context for future re-analysis.

title: Types of DNA-derived data
section: Darwin Core + DNA Derived Data Extension
text:
Darwin Core + DNA Derived Data Extension

Darwin Core: shared vocabulary for biodiversity data

DNA Extension: adds MIxS terms for sequences, primers, methods

Developed jointly by GBIF, OBIS, and the genomics community






meta.xml ← links all tables
eml.xml ← metadata (who, what, where, when, how)
occurrence.csv ← the occurrence records
dna_derived.csv ← the DNA extension



MIxS = Minimum Information about any (x) Sequence. The extension incorporates these community standards so the same fields used in INSDC/GenBank submissions can be reused here.

title: Types of DNA-derived data
text:
Core table for DNA data
DNA data can be published with either core type:

Occurrence core + DNA extension
Event core + Occurrence extension + DNA extension




This wasn’t always true


Previously guidance required Occurrence core only for DNA data. Event core support was added recently!




Both options link the DNA extension via occurrenceID. Event core additionally lets you record sample-level metadata (location, date, method) once per event instead of once per sequence — see the next slide for why that matters at scale. A caveat worth mentioning live: even with Event core, some duplication is unavoidable because of the star schema (DNA extension still needs occurrenceID, so each occurrence still carries a full sequence/sample link). A newer GBIF/OBIS data model in development aims to remove this constraint entirely, but it isn’t implemented yet (as of late 2025).

title: Types of DNA-derived data
section: Key DwC fields to include: Event
text:
Key DwC fields to include: Event


Event core (mandatory fields)

eventID — unique, stable ID
eventDate — YYYY-MM-DD
decimalLatitude, decimalLongitude



Occurrence table (strongly recommended fields)

minimumDepthInMeters, maximumDepthInMeters
sampleSizeValue (volume of sample)

sampleSizeUnit (units of volume sampled)
samplingProtocol - (link to your field sampling SOP)

title: Types of DNA-derived data
section: Key DwC fields to include: Occurrence
text:
Key DwC fields to include: Occurrence


Occurrence (core) table (mandatory fields)

occurrenceID — unique, stable ID
scientificName — WoRMS ID
basisOfRecord = “MaterialSample”

occurrenceStatus = “present”

If Occurrence core, also: eventDate, decimalLatitude, decimalLongitude

Occurrence table (strongly recommended fields)

scientificNameID (WoRMS AphiaID)

organismQuantity (read count of sequence)

associatedSequences (link to e.g. GenBank or ENA accession)
organismQuantityType = “DNA sequence reads”
sampleSizeValue (total read count in sample)

sampleSizeUnit = “DNA sequence reads”
samplingProtocol - (link to your SOP)

title: Types of DNA-derived data
section: Key fields to include: DNA Extension
text:
Key fields to include: DNA Extension


DNA Extension (example key fields)

DNA_sequence — ASV/OTU sequence
target_gene — e.g. “COI”, “18S rRNA”
pcr_primer_name_forward — and reverse
seq_meth — e.g. “Illumina MiSeq”
otu_class_appr — e.g. “DADA2 v1.18”
otu_db — reference database
sop — link to your protocol


 https://manual.obis.org/dna_data.html#quick-start-guide


WoRMS = World Register of Marine Species. Every marine occurrence should have a WoRMS AphiaID as the taxonID. For freshwater / terrestrial taxa use the appropriate backbone (CoL, etc.). Unidentified sequences should still be included — they can be assigned taxon later.

occurrenceID
measurementType
measurementValue
measurementUnit





YEARsite1samp1

temperature
25
C












measurementTypeID
measurementUnitID





http://vocab.nerc.ac.uk/collection/P01/current/TEMPPR01/
http://vocab.nerc.ac.uk/collection/P06/current/UPAA/





As some of us may already be familiar, eMoF is the extension table that holds ALL kinds of measurement or fact data including measurements on organisms, information about the sampling gear used, or environmental variables measured. A reminder that this data is organized in LONG format. All the measurements are organized in rows within the columns measurementType, measurementValue, and measurementUnit

title: Types of DNA-derived data
text:
Controlled Vocabulary


Identifiers for each eMoF column

measurementUnitID
measurementValueID

non-numeric values

measurementTypeID

→ Facilitates data understanding
→ Enables data aggregation
→ Decreases misuse potential

What should vocabulary terms capture? 

OBIS recommends using NERC vocabulary terms



Emof lets you attach vocabulary identifiers to each of the emof columns. This makes our measurement data are more easily comparable and interoperable, more easily aggregated for analysis, and can decrease misuse potential!
When selecting a vocab it’s important to think about what information needs to be included in the definition for the users to understand your measurement so you should consider the property/attribute (e.g. length, concentration), what’s the object/entity of interest (a organism, a chemical), whether enviro context is important, and whether a user needs to know the methods used to properly understand the measurement OBIS recommends the NERC vocabular collection and the OBIS manual has much more detail on controlled vocabulary

title: Accessing OBIS data
text:
Getting data out of OBIS
Several ways in, depending on what you need:

obis.org — browse, search, and cite datasets
OBIS Mapper — visual exploration + quick downloads
robis — programmatic access from R
Parquet + robisdb (new) — fast, local access to the full dataset


This mirrors the options from Module 8, but we’re focusing on the four most relevant for this group. Full CSV exports and direct IPT downloads exist too, but the four above cover most real workflows. Good moment to ask the room what they’re trying to do (one species vs. a whole region vs. bulk analysis) since that determines which of these fits best — the next few slides map roughly to “small ad hoc pull” → “bulk/programmatic.”

title: Accessing OBIS data
text:
The OBIS portal — obis.org


Your starting point for finding and understanding data:

Search & browse by taxon, region, or dataset
View dataset summary + citation info
Find data provider’s contact details





Worth contrasting with the Mapper (next slide): downloads from the homepage/dataset page come as separate table files, while Mapper/full-export downloads are flattened into one occurrence+event table, with eMoF/DNA extensions available only if you check the box for them. This distinction trips people up, so flag it explicitly.

title: Accessing OBIS data
text:
OBIS Mapper — mapper.obis.org


Visual, filter-driven exploration of OBIS data:

Filter by taxon, geography, time, and extensions
Combine multiple layers in one view
Download individual layers as CSV

Better for smaller datasets <20,000,000 records

Incldue eMoF and/or DNA-derived data extensions

(check the box — not on by default)






Great tool for a quick visual gut-check before doing anything programmatic — “does this region actually have eDNA data in it?” The Maptool naming collision is a real source of confusion, worth stating explicitly since Maptool came up earlier in the sensitive-data context.

title: Accessing OBIS data
text:
robis — programmatic access from R
install.packages("robis")
library(robis)

records <- occurrence(scientificname = "Megaptera novaeangliae", fields="associatedSequences")
dna <- dataset(scientificname = "Megaptera novaeangliae", fields="associatedSequences")

Query by species, taxon group, geometry (WKT), depth, time, quality flags…
Built-in checklist() for taxon lists in an area
dataset() — query for datasets instead of biological records

github.com/iobis/robis

This is the go-to for most “I need this species/region’s data in R” tasks — talks directly to the OBIS API under the hood, so no local storage needed. Good for small-to-medium pulls; for anything approaching “the whole OBIS dataset” or repeated heavy queries, the next slide’s Parquet approach is a better fit.

title: Accessing OBIS data
text:
New: fast access via Parquet

OBIS publishes the full global dataset as Parquet fields
Brand-new R package, robisdb

(released this month — actively evolving)


pak::pak("iobis/robisdb")
library(robisdb)

con <- connect_opendata_local("~/data/obis-open-data")
occ <- con |> occurrence_db(scientificname = "Minuca rapax")

Mirrors robis functions where possible (occurrence_db(), checklist_db()…)
Runs on DuckDB — fast, local queries without loading everything into memory
Built for scale: full-dataset or repeated analyses that would be slow via the API


This is very new (this month), so treat it as “here’s where things are heading” rather than a fully mature, documented workflow — check github.com/iobis/robisdb for the latest since details may shift. Good to mention this exists specifically because loading the entire OBIS dataset via the API/robis isn’t feasible on most laptops — Parquet + DuckDB is OBIS’s answer to that scale problem. Related datasets (speciesgrids, obistherm) are also queryable the same way.

title: FAIR Data Principles & Biodiversity Standards
text:
What makes data FAIR?





Descriptive metadata with stable links (e.g. DOI)
Clear instructions on how to access

Not just “available upon request”

Easily combined with other datasets & machine readable
Metadata enables replication

Data is contextualized




This is the “why” before we get into the “how” (Darwin Core, EOVs, sensitive data). Starting with a quick overview on what actually makes data FAIR. To summarize each aspect – Findable means that there is descriptive metadata with stable links and identifiers e.g. DOI that enable finding our data Accessible means that there are clear instructions on how to access our data, going beyond ‘available upon request’ statements Interoperable means that our data can be easily combined with other datasets and that our data & metadata are machine readable. This often means adhering to data standards and using controlled vocabulary whenever possible Reusable means that the metadata is documented well enough to enable others to replicate what we’ve done and there is enough context so that users know what situations our data can be reused in (e.g looking back on data collected 20 yrs ago, code)

title: FAIR Data Principles & Biodiversity Standards
text:
What can FAIR data do?


Invasive species decision tool & alert system portal.pacman.obis.org





Before I talk more about data standards, let’s look at some examples that demonstrate the power of FAIR data This first example is work that was done through the PacMAN project (Pacific islands Marine bioinvasions Alert Network), where our team developed a decision tool & alert system for detecting invasive species. The tool uses data from OBIS AND GBIF, including dna derived detections and other types of observations. When a pest species is detected, metadata about the detection is used to determine the detection confidence and confirm its status, if it’s a real detection. This allows informed decisions to be made on how to deal with the bioinvasion This last screenshot shows the tool and where you can see the confidence and status of detections

title: FAIR Data Principles & Biodiversity Standards
section: How to manage and make data FAIR?
text:
How to manage and make data FAIR?















How do we make data FAIR - OBIS + DwC standard!

title: FAIR Data Principles & Biodiversity Standards
text:
What is OBIS?


Ocean Biodiversity Information System

Global open-access data system for marine life

UNESCO Intergovernmental Oceanographic Commission

> 199 M occurrence records from >7,000 datasets

Data flows to GBIF — easy to publish once, reach both

Free to use. Free to contribute.


🌐 obis.org
📖 manual.obis.org


OBIS is endorsed by UNESCO’s IOC. Data published there can flow to GBIF as well, giving you double visibility with a single submission. Emphasize that OBIS is not just a repository but actively feeds into global biodiversity assessments, climate analyses, and conservation planning.

title: FAIR Data Principles & Biodiversity Standards
text:
A Mature Global Community







OBIS is the world’s largest open-access repositoryfor marine biodiversity data.
 

36 nodes  across 99 countries  representing 1,000+ institutions  and 6,000+ scientists.




OBIS sits within UNESCO’s IOC (IODE, est. 1961), working alongside ODIS for interoperability and OTGA for training. Strong partnerships include GBIF, TDWG, EMODnet, GOOS, and DataONE.
Current stats: 136M species observations, 12M records from ABNJ, 28M DNA sequences (Jan 2026).
This data supports multiple agreements: Paris Agreement, 30x30, GBF (Targets 3, 6, 8, 20, 21), SDG14, and now BBNJ.
Recognition: CBD COP16 recognized OBIS, GOOS, and ODIS as ocean components of the Global Biodiversity Observing System.
OBIS is listed in KMGBF Targets 20 and 21.
OBIS data supports: IPBES Global Assessment, UN World Ocean Assessments, UNESCO IOC Global HAB reports, State of the Ocean reports, TWAP Open Ocean component (UNEP), and CBD EBSA.

title: FAIR Data Principles & Biodiversity Standards
text:
OBIS & FAIR Data









What OBIS gives you




Findable
Your dataset joins a global, searchable index - with a DOI


Accessible
Standard download/API access, no one-off email requests


Interoperable
Darwin Core structure - readable by people and machines


Reusable
Documented metadata + a clear reuse license (e.g. CC-BY)










A real example


A marine invasive species dataset from Venezuela, published July 2022, was included in 1,873 separate download requests within 3 months — with zero extra effort from the data provider!




…it’s the reason people don’t have to keep emailing you for your spreadsheet.

title: FAIR Data Principles & Biodiversity Standards
section: Do you use a standard to format your data?
text:
Do you use a standard to format your data?
¿Utiliza algún estándar para formatear los datos?

title: FAIR Data Principles & Biodiversity Standards
text:
What is Darwin Core?



Stable, shared vocabulary for biodiversity data

Standardized field names, identifiers, & definitions

Maintained by TDWG, used by OBIS, GBIF, and the wider biodiversity community
Not a database itself - a set of agreed-upon terms (e.g. scientificName, eventDate, decimalLatitude)









Think of it as everyone agreeing to call the same column the same thing - so your data and someone else’s can sit in the same table!

🤝



For folks who’ve never touched DwC: it’s just a controlled list of column names and what they mean, nothing more mystical than that. For the DwC veterans in the room, this is review — we’re about to build on this with how those terms get organized into tables (next slide), which matters a lot once we get into DNA data this afternoon/tomorrow.

title: FAIR Data Principles & Biodiversity Standards
text:
Flat vs. relational data tables


Flat file
One giant table - everything repeated on every row



date
site
lat
lon
species
count




2024-01
A
11
-74
fish1
3


2024-01
A
11
-74
fish2
1


2024-01
A
11
-74
fish3
7




Relational
Separate, linked tables — shared info stored once
site.csv:      site, date, lat, lon
species.csv:   site, species, count
🔗 Linked by a shared identifier (an ID column)


This is the concept underlying Darwin Core Archives: a “core” table (e.g. Event) and one or more “extension” tables (e.g. Occurrence, DNA extension) linked by eventID/occurrenceID. It reduces repetition and errors — fix the date once in the Event table instead of on every one of a thousand species rows. Keep this fast for people who already know relational databases; it’s here mainly to set up tomorrow’s DNA data session where we’ll have Event → Occurrence → DNA extension as three linked tables.

title: FAIR Data Principles & Biodiversity Standards
text:
Core & Extension tables



Every Darwin Core Archive has one core table: either Event or Occurrence
Plus zero, one, or more extension tables (eMoF, DNA extension…)
Extensions link back to the core via an ID














This is the exact structure we’ll use for DNA-derived data later — so if this clicks now, the DNA session will feel familiar rather than brand new. This “star schema” (extensions only link to the core, not to each other) is a real constraint of the Darwin Core Archive format, which is why OBIS built eMoF as a workaround — but that’s a detail for later, not needed here.

title: FAIR Data Principles & Biodiversity Standards
text:
A note on sensitive data


Common cases:

Endangered or commonly poached species locations
Species/locations with economic sensitivity

Options → you don’t have to choose between “share everything” or “share nothing”:

Generalize location - provide a region or polygon (footprintWKT) instead of an exact point
Delay publication - prepare to publish but set an embargo for later
Generalize taxonomic name - provide a higher taxon name




Note: Generalizations should be documented alongside your data! See OBIS Manual & GBIF Guide for more details

Sensitivity is not a reason to skip publishing. There’s a spectrum of options short of full open release. GBIF also has a good best-practices doc on generalizing sensitive species data if anyone wants to go deeper (Chapman 2020, GBIF Secretariat). There are tools for generalizing locations (OBIS Map Tool, Marine Regions Gazetteer) in the OBIS manual + GBIF guide

title: FAIR Data Principles & Biodiversity Standards
text:
Essential Ocean Variables (EOVs)



Defined by GOOS (Global Ocean Observing System, IOC-UNESCO)

Minimum set of variables needed to track ocean state and change

36 EOVs → physical, biogeochemical, biological & ecosystem
Specification sheets to guide data collection




 






Why this matters


Well-structured data can directly feed EOV assessments used for global reporting and policy (e.g. UN Ocean Decade, conservation targets)




GOOS Biology & Ecosystems (BioEco) Panel defines the biological/ecosystem EOVs. OBIS is a key data source these assessments draw on. You don’t need to memorize the EOV list — the point is that standardized, well-formatted data (what the rest of today covers) is what makes this pipeline work at all. If useful, mention the EOV Metadata App (eovmetadata.obis.org) as a lightweight related tool, but it’s a separate system from the IPT we’ll cover this afternoon.

title: FAIR Data Principles & Biodiversity Standards
text:
Why standardize data?

Enables data to be found, combined, and reused across research groups, institutions, and countries
Avoids duplicated effort — no need to re-collect or re-process data that already exists elsewhere
Supports large-scale synthesis (e.g. global biodiversity assessments, ocean health indicators) that no single dataset could achieve alone
Keeps data accessible and usable long after a project ends or funding runs out
Makes data machine-readable, so pipelines and tools can integrate it automatically instead of manual reformatting
Provides context via metadata so others can correctly interpret and trust the data
Maximizes the return on investment of data collection, which is often costly (fieldwork, sequencing, expert time)


To summarzize… standardization isn’t just for its own sake — it’s what lets individual datasets add up to something bigger than the sum of their parts, and what protects the investment already made in collecting the data.

title: Publishing Data to OBIS
text:
Publishing workflow
Integrated Publishing Toolkits (IPT) are hosted by OBIS nodes — you do not need to run your own server. OBIS has nodes covering most world regions. Find yours at obis.org/nodes.




OBIS has nodes covering most world regions. Find yours at obis.org/nodes. The IPT has a built-in metadata editor for EML so you do not need to write XML by hand. Emphasize that once published, the dataset gets a DOI and can be cited in papers.

title: Publishing Data to OBIS
text:
Before exporting files
✅ QC checks passed
Quick self-check before moving on:

No duplicate occurrenceID/eventID
Coordinates & dates validated
Taxonomy matched against WoRMS
Required fields complete


This is a deliberately light recap — the room already did QC in an earlier session, this is just to make sure nobody’s about to export a file that still has issues. Keep this fast; the point is the transition from “QC done” to “now let’s package it,” not re-teaching QC.

title: Publishing Data to OBIS
text:
What files are needed?
Every dataset = 1 core table + 0 or more extensions
For eDNA data → typically minimum of 3 DwC files:





Plus emof.csv for environmental/biotic measurements!

This is a good moment to have people physically check their own folder against this list before moving forward — “do you have 3 files, correctly named, sitting in one place?” Occurrence-core-only datasets (no Event core) would be just 2 files (occurrence + dna_derived) — worth a quick check-in on which structure people chose back in the core-table slide.

title: Publishing Data to OBIS
text:
Before you publish: term checklist


Required

eventID (if using Event core)
occurrenceID
eventDate
decimalLatitude, decimalLongitude
scientificName (+ scientificNameID/AphiaID)
occurrenceStatus
basisOfRecord


Strongly recommended

organismQuantity + organismQuantityType
samplingProtocol
coordinateUncertaintyInMeters
associatedSequences (DNA data)



This deliberately echoes the key-fields slides from earlier — the goal is to give people one clean pre-flight checklist right before they touch the IPT. If a field’s missing, the IPT mapping step will surface it as unmapped, but it’s much less stressful to catch it beforehand.

title: Publishing Data to OBIS
text:
Preparing your files

Format: delimited text (.csv or .txt preferred) → not .xlsx
Encoding: save as UTF-8

matters especially for accented/special characters!

File names: short, no spaces or special characters → matches your table role (event.csv, occurrence.csv…)
Column names: mapped to Darwin Core terms
Large files? zip them first — IPT can unzip archives!







Saving as UTF-8


Excel: Save As → “CSV UTF-8 (Comma delimited)”
Numbers (Mac): File → Export To → CSV → Advanced Options → Unicode (UTF-8)




The 100MB IPT upload limit is per file, not per archive — zipping helps but if a single table is genuinely huge, mention there are workarounds (database connection, chunked upload) documented in the IPT manual, not something to solve live here.

title: Publishing Data to OBIS
text:
A head start on metadata
Drafting metadata beforehand saves time!
Have ready before the IPT session:

Title, abstract, citation text
Contact + creator names & affiliations, ORCiDs
Sampling methodology text or links
Relevant project information (name, identifier)
Funding or other acknowledgement statements
Keywords, license choice (CC0 / CC-BY)

The IPT has a built-in metadata editor you can paste information into

Keep this light — the goal is just “come to the IPT session with a doc of these answers ready to paste in,” not teaching EML XML structure. We’ll cover the metadata editor itself, and what’s technically required vs. recommended, in the IPT walkthrough.

title: Publishing Data to OBIS
text:
Looking ahead: automatic pipelines
Manual export → IPT works well for most datasets — especially one-off or periodically-updated ones
But…



Good to know


For institutions publishing continuously or at scale, you can set up a pipeline that pushes formatted data from an internal database → Darwin Core → the IPT via the API




This is a “know it exists” pointer, not a how-to — if it’s relevant to your institution, your node manager is the right first contact. Flagging this honestly as something to explore later rather than teaching it live — good to mention institutions with recurring monitoring data (e.g. long-term time series) sometimes go this route, but it’s a bigger lift (needs IPT API/database connection setup) than what we can responsibly cover in this workshop.

title: Publishing Data to OBIS
text:
Getting started on the IPT


1. Contact node mananager to create IPT account
2. Log in to account
3. Manage resources → Create new → (= your dataset)

Give it a short, unique shortname (no spaces/special characters — this can’t be changed later!)
Choose a type: Occurrence or Sampling-Event










Testing?


Add _test to the end of your shortname, and let your node manager know before you delete it.




Shortnames become part of the resource’s URL and folder structure, hence the “can’t change it later” warning. Type selection here maps directly to what we covered this morning: Occurrence core vs. Event core. We’ll create a real (test) resource together in the live demo.

title: Publishing Data to OBIS
text:
Uploading & mapping your data


Upload: Overview → Source Data → Choose File
Map: for each source file, click + Add and match columns to Darwin Core terms

Double check IPT’s auto-matches
Unmapped fields sit at bottom — check if they need to be included or leave them (they just won’t be published)
Repeat for each table: Event, Occurrence, DNA extension, eMoF…








Watch for “redundant terms”


If a field like eventDate appears in both Occurrence and Event, the IPT will flag it - pick one table for it!




This is the step where all the field-mapping work from this morning (the key-fields slides) actually gets applied. Good moment to remind people: if they did the “map your columns to DwC terms in a documentation sheet” prep work already, this step is mostly mechanical.

title: Publishing Data to OBIS
text:
Filling in metadata
OBIS/GBIF use Ecological Metadata Language (EML) — filled in through the IPT’s metadata editor, no XML needed by hand!
Bare minimum required:

Title
Description (abstract)
License
Contact
Citation

Also worth completing: keywords, sampling methods, project information, geographic/temporal/taxonomic coverage

Good metadata is what makes your dataset discoverable and understandable (FAIR) — think about what you’d want to know before reusing a dataset


Only 4 fields are technically required, but strongly encourage completing more — geographic/temporal/taxonomic coverage in particular makes a dataset much easier to find via OBIS’s search/filter tools. This is filled in via Resource Overview → Metadata → Edit on the IPT, which we’ll show live.

title: Publishing Data to OBIS
text:
Choosing a data license



License
Meaning




CC0 (preferred)
No restrictions, full public domain


CC-BY
Free use, with attribution required


CC-BY-NC
Free non-commercial use, with attribution



Check with your institution if there are copyright concerns before choosing.

OBIS lists these in order of preference — CC0 is preferred because it maximizes reuse/interoperability, but CC-BY is very common and totally fine. This is a quick, low-drama slide; most participants will already know which one their institution requires.

title: Publishing Data to OBIS
text:
Publishing & keeping it updated
Publish → packages data + metadata into a Darwin Core Archive (.zip)



⚠️ Publishing ≠ public!


Visibility defaults to Private. Set Visibility → Public when ready



After publishing:

A DOI can be assigned to your dataset for citation
Datasets are versioned — re-upload + republish to update

Old versions stay accessible (&v=n in the URL)

Never delete + recreate a resource to “start over” — just publish a new version!


This is the step people get nervous about — reassure them the Private → Public toggle is a deliberate safety net, and test datasets should just get _test in the shortname and a heads-up to the node manager rather than being made public. Versioning means mistakes are very recoverable — nothing is destroyed by re-publishing.

title: Publishing Data to OBIS
text:
Publish once, reach both networks
Your dataset can also be registered with GBIF — no separate upload needed!



Publication section, set Registration → Registered
Not required for OBIS — OBIS already harvests your IPT independently
Skip if dataset is already published in GBIF by another publisher

Ideally there is only one IPT resource per dataset

IPT itself must also be registered with GBIF (ask your node manager)










Publish once, use many times


OBIS and GBIF collaborate to ensure biodiversity data reaches both networks. Data providers only need to upload once to reach both!




Good moment to name the OBIS/GBIF relationship explicitly: OBIS focuses on marine data with WoRMS as its taxonomic backbone, GBIF is broader biodiversity with Catalog of Life — but they share the same underlying IPT infrastructure and Darwin Core standard. Worth flagging one real gap though: OBIS-specific extensions (eMoF, DNA-derived data) aren’t currently included when a dataset is viewed/downloaded through GBIF — so eDNA-specific detail may not carry over even though the core occurrence data does.

title: Publishing Data to OBIS
text:
Let’s see it live
We’ll now walk through a real IPT instance together:

Create a (test) resource
Upload source data & map to Darwin Core
Fill in minimum metadata
Publish → set visibility → done ✅
Add to GBIF network!


This slide is just a checkpoint to move into live demo

title: Mapping molecular data to Darwin Core
section: Identifying key information for Metabarcoding & qPCR pipelines
text: Identifying key information for Metabarcoding & qPCR pipelines

title: Mapping molecular data to Darwin Core
text:
eDNA Metabarcoding: Key Information
Category 1 — DNA-derived occurrences — basisOfRecord = MaterialSample
From field collection to taxonomy — what to capture at each step
Step 1:⛵ Field Sampling







Field Sampling
Maps to




Coordinates, location names
Event table: decimalLatitude, decimalLongitude, locality


Date, time
Event table: eventDate, eventTime


Environmental measurements (temperature, salinity, depth…)
eMoF table: measurementType, measurementValue, measurementUnit


Sampling method / gear
Event table: samplingProtocol and/or eMoF table


Sample volume, filter size
DNA table: samp_size , size_frac




This is where the Event core table gets populated. Environmental measurements that aren’t DNA-specific (e.g. temperature, salinity) go in eMoF rather than the DNA extension — worth a quick reminder here since people sometimes want to cram everything into one table.

title: Mapping molecular data to Darwin Core
text:
eDNA Metabarcoding: Key Information
Category 1 — DNA-derived occurrences — basisOfRecord = MaterialSample
From field collection to taxonomy — what to capture at each step
Step 2: 🧬 DNA Extraction







DNA Extraction
Maps to




Volume of sample processed
DNA table: samp_vol_we_dna_ext


Extraction method / kit
DNA table: nucl_acid_ext, nucl_acid_amp


Concentration
DNA table: concentration, concentrationUnit


Purity ratio (A260/280)
DNA table: ratioOfAbsorbance260_280




Extraction method matters a lot for reproducibility and comparing datasets — different kits can bias which taxa get amplified downstream, so it’s worth capturing even though it’s easy to skip. Purity ratio (260/280) is a quick QC signal for contamination that’s worth flagging even if participants don’t record it religiously yet.

title: Mapping molecular data to Darwin Core
text:
eDNA Metabarcoding: Key Information
Category 1 — DNA-derived occurrences — basisOfRecord = MaterialSample
From field collection to taxonomy — what to capture at each step
Step 3: 🧪 DNA Amplification







DNA Amplification
Maps to (DNA table)




Target gene (e.g. COI, 18S rRNA)
DNA table: target_gene


Primer sequence + primer reference
DNA table: pcr_primer_forward, pcr_primer_name_forward, pcr_primer_reverse, pcr_primer_name_reverse + pcr_primer_reference


PCR conditions
DNA table: pcr_cond


Length of amplified product
DNA table: ampliconSize




Primer choice determines which taxa can even be detected — always cite the primer reference, don’t just paste the sequence, so users can trace back to the original validation. PCR conditions (annealing temp, cycle number) belong here too even though they feel like lab-notebook detail; they matter for anyone trying to reproduce or compare your results.

title: Mapping molecular data to Darwin Core
text:
eDNA Metabarcoding: Key Information
Category 1 — DNA-derived occurrences — basisOfRecord = MaterialSample
From field collection to taxonomy — what to capture at each step
Step 4: 💻 Sequences







Sequences
Maps to




Sequencing platform + library layout
DNA table: seq_meth + lib_layout (DNA table)


Sequences per sample
DNA table: DNA_sequence (linked by occurrenceID)


Public repository where sequences deposited (NCBI, ENA)
Occurrence table: associatedSequences




This is the last step before formatting — everything captured across the last 3 slides plus this one feeds into either the Occurrence table or the DNA extension. Reference database + pipeline (e.g. “DADA2 v1.18” + “SILVA 138”) matter a lot for reproducibility since taxonomy assignments can shift as databases update — this is what otu_class_appr and otu_db are for.

title: Mapping molecular data to Darwin Core
text:
eDNA Metabarcoding: Key Information
Category 1 — DNA-derived occurrences — basisOfRecord = MaterialSample
From field collection to taxonomy — what to capture at each step
Step 5: 🐠 Taxonomy







Taxonomy
Maps to




Taxonomy assignment + annotation confidence
Occurrence table: scientificName, taxonID  DNA table: annot


Reference database + pipeline used
Occurrence table: identificationRemarks, identificationReferences  DNA table: otu_db + sop


Number of sequence reads per taxon & total
Occurence table: organismQuantity, organismQuantityType, & sampleSizeValue, sampleSizeUnit




This is the last step before formatting — everything captured across the last 3 slides plus this one feeds into either the Occurrence table or the DNA extension. Reference database + pipeline (e.g. “DADA2 v1.18” + “SILVA 138”) matter a lot for reproducibility since taxonomy assignments can shift as databases update — this is what otu_class_appr and otu_db are for.

title: Mapping molecular data to Darwin Core
text:
Key Metadata: Quantitative PCR (qPCR)
Category 3 — Targeted species detection — basisOfRecord = MaterialSample
Targeted species detection — additional fields vs metabarcoding



⛵ Field Sampling → same as metabarcoding
🧬 DNA Extraction → same as metabarcoding
🧪 DNA Amplification → PCR conditions → pcr_cond
🎯 Target & Quantity







Target & Quantity
Maps to




Target gene region
target_gene, target_subfragment


Primers
pcr_primers


Copy number + concentration
estimatedNumberOfCopies + concentration, concentrationUnit


LOD, Cq, baseline
pcr_primer_lod, quantificationCycle, baselineValue




qPCR = Category 3 — targeted species detection. Adds qPCR-specific fields: probeReporter, probeQuencher, estimatedNumberOfCopies, thresholdQuantificationCycle, and baselineValue. Key difference from metabarcoding: you are detecting one specific taxon, so scientificName and scientificNameID are required from the start. Use basisOfRecord = MaterialSample.
